lesson.menu_desc: Writing for journals: the academic register of abstracts, papers and peer review -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Abstracts & Papers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 16 -- Abstracts & Papers

listenings.text: Welcome, and thank you for joining this short session on writing for international journals. Before you submit a manuscript, it helps to understand how editors and reviewers read your work. The first thing they see is the synopsis at the top of the paper. It must be short, usually under three hundred words, and it should state the premise of the study, the methods, the main results, and the conclusion. Editors decide in a few minutes whether your work fits their journal, so this short text carries a lot of weight. After the editor accepts the paper for review, it goes to two or three referees, who are experts in your field. They read it carefully and write a report with comments and questions. This is the heart of the process: it is the way the scientific community checks that your results are reliable. When you describe other studies in your text, always add a citation, so readers can find the original source. And remember, a clear, formal register matters. Avoid casual words, use the passive voice where it sounds natural, and let the data speak. A well written paper, with a strong synopsis and careful citations, is far more likely to pass review and reach publication.

Hi Patricia, this is the editor of the Journal of Clinical Nutrition. Good news: your manuscript has passed the first check and we are now sending it out for review. Two referees have agreed to read it, and we expect their reports in about four weeks. In the meantime, I have one small request about the synopsis at the top. It is very clear, but it does not mention the sample size, and reviewers usually look for that. Could you add one short sentence with the number of patients and the main result? Also, please double check your citations against our style guide before the next submission, because a few of them use the wrong format. Everything else looks strong, and the premise of the study is well argued. We are glad you chose our journal, and we look forward to the next version of the paper. Thank you, and talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, state the premise of the study in one sentence. Next, describe the methods you used. After that, present the main results. Then, give your conclusion. Finally, keep the whole synopsis under three hundred words.
